children.name: Podiceps
children.description: The large grebes — superbly adapted diving birds with lobed feet set so far back on the body that they are virtually unable to walk on land. Grebes are among the most accomplished divers of all freshwater birds, using their feet rather than wings to propel themselves underwater in pursuit of fish. The great crested grebe's elaborate courtship display — the most complex of any Swedish waterbird — involves both partners rising chest-to-chest from the water in the 'penguin dance', shaking their spectacular rufous-and-black crests and exchanging gifts of waterweed. This ceremony was first scientifically described by Julian Huxley in 1914 and remains a textbook example of ritualized courtship behaviour. The grebes' beautiful plumage made them targets for the 19th-century feather trade; their recovery catalysed early British bird protection legislation.

children.name: Podiceps cristatus
children.description: The skäggdopping — Sweden's largest grebe and one of its most beautiful birds. The elaborate head-shaking courtship dance in early spring, performed by both sexes with erect rufous-and-black crests, is one of the most spectacular sights on Swedish lakes. Nests on sheltered bays of larger lakes; chicks ride on the parents' backs beneath the wing feathers. Once hunted nearly to extinction for its silky breast plumage, which was used to trim Victorian hats; now common and increasing across lowland Sweden.

children.name: Podiceps grisegena
children.description: The gråhakedopping — a declining breeding species in southern and central Sweden, requiring larger eutrophic lakes with rich fish populations. In breeding plumage its chestnut-red neck, grey cheeks, and yellow bill base make it unmistakeable. Less showy in courtship than its great crested relative but equally devoted to its mate, with pairs duetting in loud braying calls across breeding lakes. Winters on sheltered Swedish coastal bays and the southern Baltic.

children.name: Podiceps auritus
children.description: The svarthakedopping — arguably the most stunning of all Swedish waterbirds in breeding plumage, with brilliant golden-orange ear-tufts that fan out from behind the eye like flames against a black cap. A scarce breeder in northern Sweden, particularly in Lapland where it nests on small forest pools and lochans. Winters on the open sea off the Swedish coast, where it is drably grey and white. Population trends are of conservation concern across its Scandinavian range.

children.name: Tachybaptus
children.description: Little grebes — the smallest and most compact of all grebes, found on a much wider range of water bodies than their larger relatives. The little grebe (smådopping) is a remarkably buoyant bird, riding high on the water like a cork and diving suddenly with a smooth, almost silent plunge. Despite its tiny size it is feisty and territorial, driving away intruders many times its own size with a loud, shrill whinny. Little grebes are one of the most widespread wetland birds in Sweden, found on ponds as small as a few hundred square metres — anywhere there is aquatic vegetation for nesting and small fish or invertebrates to eat. In autumn they can aggregate in small flocks on larger lakes before moving to coastal wintering areas.

children.name: Tachybaptus ruficollis
children.description: The smådopping — Europe's smallest grebe, barely larger than a starling. Its rotund, tailless silhouette and habit of bouncing back to the surface after each dive make it instantly recognisable. Surprisingly widespread and common in Sweden, nesting on ponds, ditches, slow rivers, and urban park lakes. Its loud, descending whinny — a cascading trill audible across the whole wetland — is one of the characteristic sounds of a Swedish spring marsh.
